Paper summary:

```markdown
# Validity Extraction: LTZGLUE: The First Natural Language Understanding Benchmark for Luxembourgish
<!-- Model routing: Haiku (per-page extraction) → script (registry assembly) → Sonnet (narrative) -->

## Metadata
- **Title**: LTZGLUE: The First Natural Language Understanding Benchmark for Luxembourgish
- **Authors**: Alistair Plum, Felicia Körner, Anne-Marie Lutgen, Laura Bernardy, Fred Philippy, Emilia Milano, Nils Rehlinger, Cédric Lothritz, Tharindu Ranasinghe, Barbara Plank, Christoph Purschke
- **Venue/Year**: Not explicitly stated in registry (inferred ~2025–2026 from citation patterns)
- **Total Pages**: 17
- **Quotes Extracted**: 159

## Narrative Context

Interpretive prose organized by extraction category. Each factual claim references quote IDs from the registry. **This section is non-authoritative — it provides readability but is not evidence. Only the Quote Registry contains verbatim text from the paper.**

---

### 1. Task Taxonomy / Test Case Categories

LTZGLUE is introduced as the first unified GLUE-style NLU benchmark for Luxembourgish, comprising eight tasks [Q1, Q8] spanning binary and multi-class sentence- and token-level classification settings [Q11]. The suite covers headline acceptability (HA), sentiment analysis (SA), linguistic acceptability in both binary and multi-class forms (LA BINARY / LA MULTI), named entity recognition (NER), topic classification (TC), intent detection (ID), and recognising textual entailment (RTE) [Q2, Q67]. From the newsroom deployment perspective, four of these eight tasks — HA, LA, TC, and NER — correspond directly to the operational pipeline; the benchmark therefore provides signal across all four deployment-relevant tasks, which reduces input-ontology risk. Headline acceptability is formulated specifically as a binary classification of whether a headline matches its article body [Q15], directly addressing flagged gap #3 and confirming this deployment-relevant subtask is in scope. Topic classification uses five domains (SPORTS, CULTURE, TECHNOLOGY, BUSINESS, ANIMALS) [Q48, Q153], which the elicitation confirms maps adequately to international standard categories, though notably the taxonomy lacks Luxembourg-specific editorial beats. The RTE task is described as the most challenging overall and ported from the original GLUE benchmark tradition [Q59, Q103], while topic classification emerges as the easiest and most stable task across all models [Q99]. Prompt templates for LLM evaluation make the task definitions explicit and confirm the label vocabularies used in zero-shot settings [Q143, Q144, Q145, Q146, Q148, Q149, Q150, Q151, Q152, Q153, Q154, Q155], and example instances are provided in Table 7 for illustration [Q124]. Together these tasks are said to place LTZGLUE in broadly the same range as the original English GLUE benchmark's nine tasks [Q68], with a substantial proportion newly created for LTZ rather than directly translated [Q69, Q70, Q71, Q108].

---

### 2. Data Sources and Collection

Most textual data across LTZGLUE tasks originates from two primary sources [Q12], with RTL (Luxembourg's dominant news broadcaster) providing news articles, radio transcripts, and user comments as a major supplier across multiple tasks [Q16, Q24, Q46, Q74, Q135]. Additional pre-training and task-specific data come from the Luxembourgish Online Dictionary (LOD) [Q30], Wikipedia [Q74], web crawls from the Leipzig Collection [Q74], Luxembourgish chat rooms [Q74], and transcribed political speeches from the Chambre des Députés [Q74]. The NER component draws on two sources: the automatically constructed JUDGEWEL corpus derived from Wikipedia and Wikidata [Q34, Q38], and a fully human-annotated corpus from RTL online news comments [Q40], which were merged to increase coverage and domain balance [Q43, Q45]. The intent detection dataset was constructed by translating the English xSID dataset into Luxembourgish, using a machine-translated German training set [Q51, Q54], and the RTE dataset originated as a machine translation of an established English corpus, subsequently post-edited with LLM assistance [Q60, Q66]. Importantly for the newsroom deployment context, the LTZ language community is noted to be small and linguistic data are concentrated in a limited set of public domains [Q119], and the benchmark construction required a deliberately resource-conscious approach combining dataset reuse with targeted annotation [Q109]. No data source in the registry is described as containing systematically code-switched text, which is a significant gap given that the deployment explicitly requires robust handling of French–German–English lexical items embedded in Luxembourgish articles [flagged gap #1].

---

### 3. Data Format and Preprocessing

All benchmark tasks use text-only inputs, consistent with the newsroom deployment's text-only pipeline. For the headline acceptability task, the dataset was constructed by pairing article bodies with swapped headlines using TF-IDF nearest-neighbour matching with temporal distancing (minimum 30-day gap) and positional similarity filtering [Q18, Q19, Q20], resulting in a 20k/3k/6k train/dev/test split [Q21], with negative examples designed to be topically related but structurally and temporally mismatched [Q22]. The linguistic acceptability dataset uses a 70-10-20 split [Q32], and NER data is encoded in BIO format using Wikipedia's hyperlink structure [Q35, Q36], with GPE and LOC categories harmonised into a single location label for cross-dataset compatibility [Q44]. Topic classification articles were filtered using OpenLID language detection and constrained to 40–400 word length, with downsampling applied to the over-represented SPORTS category [Q47, Q49, Q50]. For intent detection, BIO tags were transferred from a German dataset via token-level fuzzy matching followed by manual verification [Q57], and for RTE, CHATGPT-5.1 was used to assess and improve translated sentence pairs [Q61]. The pre-training data for the LTZ-E1 encoder models was filtered to sentences with at least three whitespace-tokenised words, yielding approximately 233M tokens across 11.7M sentences [Q75]. Model architecture details specify BPE tokenisation with a vocabulary of 50,368 [Q128] and a maximum sequence length of 1,024 [Q129], using the ModernBERT/Ettin recipe [Q125, Q126, Q127, Q130, Q131]. For the newsroom deployment, the orthographic normalisation applied during preprocessing is particularly relevant: there is no explicit statement that benchmark text reflects the flexible journalistic register, and the filtering pipeline's language-ID step [Q47] would tend to exclude code-switched sentences, compounding the monolingual bias concern. Personal data handling is noted as privacy-respecting, with preprocessing avoiding directly identifying information [Q116].

---

### 4. Label Categories and Output Types

The label ontologies across LTZGLUE tasks are a mix of purpose-built and ported schemes. Linguistic acceptability uses four error subtypes — verb agreement, adjective agreement, syntax deletion, and orthographic substitution — derived from LOD dictionary data and a spellchecker resource [Q31], with the binary version collapsing all error types into a single incorrect label (0) against correct (1) [Q33]. For NER, the harmonised tag set covers PER, ORG, LOC (merged from GPE+LOC), DATE, and MISC in BIO format [Q41, Q44, Q152]; notably, this merging loses the GPE/LOC distinction that could be important for identifying cross-border entities (EU institutions, Belgian or French place names) in Luxembourgish journalism. Topic classification labels are restricted to five domains: SPORTS, CULTURE, TECHNOLOGY, BUSINESS, and ANIMALS [Q48, Q153], a deliberately narrow set that excludes political and institutional beats common in Luxembourgish professional newsrooms, though the elicitation confirms the deployment does not require bespoke regional categories. The binary label convention (0 = False, 1 = True) is made explicit for headline acceptability and textual entailment tasks [Q147], and the intent detection label set is defined for a voice-assistant domain [Q154, Q58]. From an OO validity perspective, the linguistic acceptability error taxonomy is grounded in formal grammatical and orthographic standards (using the official LOD and spellchecker.lu), which may systematically diverge from the flexible professional-journalistic register the newsroom targets: borderline cases acknowledged in the elicitation — where a formal language authority and editorial team would disagree — are not reflected in the label ontology.

---

### 5. Annotation Process

Annotation practices across LTZGLUE tasks are heterogeneous in quality and transparency. For sentiment analysis, 4,583 sentences were annotated by two native LTZ speakers, with an instruction to use an "unsure" label only when otherwise randomly choosing [Q25, Q26]; inter-annotator agreement reached a Cohen's Kappa of 0.45 [Q27], which is generally considered moderate and signals meaningful disagreement, with conflicts resolved by annotator consensus [Q28]. For the intent detection task, translation was performed by a single LTZ native speaker, with additional native speakers consulted in uncertain cases [Q52]. The JUDGEWEL NER corpus used LLMs as judges with only minimal human verification to calibrate quality thresholds [Q37], whereas the Lothritz et al. NER dataset was fully human-annotated [Q42]. For RTE, two LLM-assisted verification steps were applied: CHATGPT-5-MINI rated translation quality (removing ~25% of data rated low) [Q63] and verified label correctness (finding ~10% false labels) [Q64], with manually corrected sentences where LLM improvements had inadvertently altered semantics [Q65]. A brief acknowledgment of student assistant annotators appears in the paper [Q114], but no demographic detail is provided about any annotators' professional background. This absence is a critical validity risk for the newsroom deployment: there is no evidence that any headline acceptability or linguistic acceptability annotations were produced by professional journalists or editors applying a journalistic register, and the formal-orthographic grounding of the LA error taxonomy [Q31] further suggests the ground truth reflects linguistic prescriptivism rather than editorial practice — a direct mismatch with the deployment's OC requirements.

---

### 6. Evaluation Metrics and Output Modality

Models are evaluated by F1 scores reported across all eight tasks [Q93, Q96], with encoder-based models averaged over three runs using seeds selected via Bayesian hyperparameter search [Q89, Q137, Q138], and standard deviations reported as subscripts [Q96, Q158, Q159]. LLM evaluation uses zero-shot prompting with explicit label vocabularies rather than multiple-choice format [Q83, Q84], and outputs that are malformed or return an incorrect number of predictions are discarded before scoring [Q90, Q91]. Class-balanced loss weighted by effective sample size (beta 0.99) is applied for fine-tuning on the imbalanced sentiment analysis and linguistic acceptability tasks [Q92]. Encoder models consistently outperform prompted LLMs across most tasks [Q94, Q95, Q98], with MMBERT-base achieving the highest aggregate score with low variance [Q97, Q105], while LTZ-specific models like LUXEMBERT are particularly strong on lexically grounded tasks such as intent detection [Q101, Q106]. LLMs perform relatively better on topic classification and RTE [Q100, Q104] but struggle on intent detection [Q102]. For the deployment scenario, the evaluation framework produces per-task F1 scores [Q156], which maps naturally onto the per-task threshold-based flagging architecture; however, the benchmark reports macro-F1 only for prompted LLMs [Q96], which may obscure task-level label imbalance relevant to deployment calibration. Compute details are documented [Q133, Q140], and full hyperparameter sweep ranges are tabulated [Q142].

---

### 7. Stated Limitations

The authors are unusually candid and thorough in acknowledging limitations, many of which are directly relevant to the newsroom deployment context. The most foundational constraint is the low-resource status of Luxembourgish itself: with approximately 400,000 speakers [Q4], classified as a "scraping-by" language with almost no labelled data [Q13], most existing NLU tasks are in the news domain and comprise fewer than a thousand instances each, making evaluation unreliable [Q5, Q6]. Ongoing standardisation and vast orthographic variation [Q7] are explicitly cited as challenges, and the authors warn that "models may reproduce dominant norms while under-representing regional, sociolectal, or multilingual practices" [Q120] — a direct concern for a deployment serving a linguistically diverse newsroom where code-switching is routine. The intent detection task carries a specific register mismatch: the source English dataset uses a spoken voice-assistant register with no LTZ equivalent reference corpus, making systematic terminology verification impossible [Q55, Q56], which limits the utility of this task for the deployment scenario. The authors acknowledge that several tasks rely on domain-specific or small corpora limiting ecological validity [Q110], and that pre-training data overlap with evaluated LLMs cannot be ruled out, potentially inflating LLM performance estimates [Q111]. Coverage across registers and demographic varieties is flagged as limited, with most sources reflecting formal writing or institutional usage rather than informal or multilingual contexts [Q112] — this is precisely the gap most consequential for the code-switching deployment requirement. Annotation decisions and label distributions were influenced by resource constraints, with potential biases that "cannot be fully quantified" [Q113], and societal biases from institutional or media data sources may influence model behaviour in deployment [Q117, Q118]. Benchmark performance should not be taken as evidence of cultural or demographic coverage [Q121], harmful content cannot be guaranteed removed [Q122], and task-specific inspection is encouraged before public-facing use [Q123]. Prompt sensitivity introduces additional variability for LLM evaluations, making those results indicative rather than directly comparable to supervised scores [Q86, Q87, Q82]. Pre-training data size constraints are also acknowledged as limiting for the LTZ-E1 models [Q132], and hyperparameter sweeps were kept simple for computational feasibility, meaning reported values should not be treated as optimal [Q139, Q134].

---

### 8. Authors and Affiliations

The paper is authored by an eleven-person team with affiliations spanning four institutions: the University of Luxembourg (Plum, Lutgen, Bernardy, Philippy, Milano, Rehlinger, Purschke), LMU Munich and the Munich Center for Machine Learning (Körner, Plank), LIST Luxembourg (Lothritz), and Lancaster University UK (Ranasinghe) [Q10]. The Luxembourg-anchored majority of authors, combined with a Luxembourg-focused LLM research project (LLMs4EU, funded under the EU Digital Europe Programme) [Q115], signals strong institutional alignment between the benchmark's deployment context and its creators' professional environment. The ERC Consolidator Grant DIALECT supporting Körner and Plank [Q115] indicates expertise in dialectal and low-resource NLP, relevant to Luxembourgish's sociolinguistic variability. New encoder models (LTZ-E1-mini and LTZ-E1-base) were trained following the Ettin/ModernBERT recipe [Q72, Q73], and baseline comparisons include mBERT, MMBERT, LUXEMBERT, XLM-RoBERTa, and several large LLMs [Q77, Q78, Q79, Q80, Q81], indicating a well-resourced evaluation environment despite the low-resource language focus.
```

| Q80 | 6 | authors_affiliations | "Finally, we evaluate XLM-RoBERTa (XLM-R-base) (Conneau et al., 2020), a large-scale multilingual model trained on substantially more data and languages than MBERT-base, and commonly used as a strong reference point for multilingual NLU." |
| Q81 | 6 | authors_affiliations | "In addition to supervised encoder-based models, we evaluate a set of LLMs in a prompt-based zero-shot setting. | Q82 | 6 | stated_limitations | "None of these models are fine-tuned on LTZGLUE, although some of the text data (RTL, Wikipedia) is very likely to have been processed during training." |

| Q84 | 6 | evaluation_metrics | "We did not use a Multiple Choice Question Answering (MCQA)-setup, but provided the labels that should be used as output." |
| Q85 | 6 | evaluation_metrics | "This evaluation setting reflects the growing use of LLMs as general-purpose language understanding systems, particularly in scenarios where annotated data is scarce or unavailable." |
| Q86 | 6 | stated_limitations | "However, prompt-based evaluation introduces additional sources of variability, including prompt sensitivity and differences in instruction-following behaviour across models. | Q105 | 8 | evaluation_metrics | "First, MMBERT-base consistently achieves the strongest or near-strongest performance across almost all tasks, combining high mean F1 scores with comparatively low variance, suggesting that broad multilingual pre-training with sufficient LTZ exposure yields stable and transferable representations." |

| Q108 | 9 | task_taxonomy | "This paper makes two central contributions to LTZ NLU. First, we introduce a new benchmark that provides the first comprehensive GLUE-style evaluation suite for LTZ. Second, we present a systematic evaluation of encoder-based models and prompted large language models across all tasks, offering concrete guidance on model choice in such a low-resource setting." |
| Q109 | 9 | data_sources | "The construction of the dataset required a deliberately resource-conscious approach. In the absence of large, task-diverse annotated resources, we combine the reuse of existing datasets with the targeted annotation of new data, carefully aligning annotation schemes across tasks, and using large language models as auxiliary tools." |
| Q110 | 9 | stated_limitations | "While LTZGLUE provides the first systematic benchmark for LTZ NLU, the dataset remains constrained by the availability and scope of existing resources. Several tasks rely on relatively small or domain-specific corpora, which limits the ecological validity of the results and restricts the range of linguistic phenomena covered." |
| Q111 | 9 | stated_limitations | "In addition, some of the data sources used in this benchmark may already be included, in whole or in part, in the pre-training corpora of the large language models evaluated in this work. While the exact composition of proprietary pre-training datasets is typically not fully disclosed, this potential overlap cannot be entirely ruled out and may inflate performance estimates." |
| Q112 | 9 | stated_limitations | "Coverage across domains, registers, and demographic varieties may also be limited. LTZ displays substantial orthographic and sociolinguistic variation, yet most data sources reflect formal writing or institutional usage and therefore do not fully represent informal and multilingual contexts." |
| Q113 | 9 | stated_limitations | "Although we draw on established GLUE-style tasks, some annotation decisions and class distributions are necessarily influenced by resource constraints. | Q117 | 10 | stated_limitations | "However, some tasks draw on data originally produced in institutional or media contexts, which may reflect societal biases in representation." |
| Q118 | 10 | stated_limitations | "These patterns can influence model behaviour and should be considered when deploying systems trained on LTZGLUE." |
| Q119 | 10 | data_sources | "LTZ is a small language community, and linguistic data often originate from a limited set of public domains." |
| Q120 | 10 | stated_limitations | "As a result, models may reproduce dominant norms while under-representing regional, sociolectal, or multilingual practices." |

| Q123 | 10 | stated_limitations | "Researchers using LTZGLUE are encouraged to inspect task-specific subsets and consider downstream implications, especially in public-facing settings." |
| Q124 | 12 | task_taxonomy | "For demonstration purposes, we present an example for each task in ltzGLUE in Table 7. The examples are intended to illustrate the task formulations and typical model inputs and outputs." |
| Q125 | 12 | data_format | "We follow the Ettin recipe (Weller et al., 2026), based on ModernBERT (Warner et al., 2025), for training hyperpameters and model architecture." |
| Q126 | 12 | data_format | "We train two sizes of LTZ-E1 models, mini and base, with 68M and 110M non-embedding parameters, respectively." |
| Q127 | 12 | data_format | "LTZ-E1-mini has 19 hidden layers, a hidden size of 512, an intermediate size of 768, and 8 attention heads, whereas LTZ-E1-base has 22 hidden layers, a hidden size of 768, an intermediate size of 1152, and 12 attention heads." |
| Q128 | 12 | data_format | "Both models share a GPTNeoXTokenizerFast tokenizer (Black et al., 2022), a BPE-based tokenizer, which we train on the entire pre-training set, using a minimum frequency of two and a vocabulary size of 50,368." |
| Q129 | 12 | data_format | "We use a constant batch size of 1024 packed sequences, where both models have a max sequence length of 1024." |
| Q130 | 12 | data_format | "We follow ModernBERT (Warner et al., 2025) and Ettin (Weller et al., 2026) in using the Warmup-Stable-Decay (WSD) scheduler (Zhai et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2024), though we use a shorter warmup and decay phase of 500 batches each, due to our smaller pre-training dataset size and larger number of epochs (10 vs. one)." |
| Q131 | 12 | data_format | "Again following ModernBERT and Ettin's recipe, we use the StableAdamW optimizer (Wortsman et al., 2023), with a peak learning rate of 3e-3 with a weight decay of 3e-4 for LTZ-E1-mini and 8e-4 with a weight decay of 1e-5 for LTZ-E1-base." |
| Q132 | 12 | stated_limitations | "As our pre-training set is small, we" |
| Q133 | 13 | evaluation_metrics | "We use a 20GB MIG partition of an NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB to pretrain each model, taking 47 hours for LTZ-E1-mini and 76 hours for LTZ-E1-base." |
| Q134 | 13 | stated_limitations | "However, we note that compute times were negatively impacted by concurrent jobs on the server cluster with suboptimal CPU thread management." |

| Q136 | 13 | evaluation_metrics | "Though we do not aim to optimise performance in our evaluation, we conduct basic hyperparameter sweeps for each model and task combination in order to provide a fairer comparison across models." |
| Q137 | 13 | evaluation_metrics | "For each model and task combination, we select the best hyperparameters based on the validation set, and use those parameters to finetune two additional models with differing seeds, resulting in three runs." |
| Q138 | 13 | evaluation_metrics | "In order to reduce the computational demand of the sweeps, we use Bayesian search with early stopping after three iterations, and cap each sweep at 30 runs, for 1,440 total runs across all models and tasks (and an additional 96 to finetune the two additional seeds)." |
| Q139 | 13 | stated_limitations | "However, we note again that these ranges were kept simple to keep sweeps computationally feasible, thus, these values should not be seen as optimal hyperparameters." |
